question_title: Will the U.S. government formally notify Congress of a new arms sale to Taiwan between May 17 and May 31, 2026?
summary: The probability of a new Taiwan FMS Congressional notification between May 17-31, 2026 is very low (~4%). The $14B package — the only major candidate — was formally paused on May 22 by acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao, citing Iran war munitions concerns. Trump has personally expressed indecision, treats the sale as a bargaining chip with China, and the post-summit diplomatic dynamics argue against action. No other Taiwan sale appears in the pipeline, no notifications have occurred in 5+ months, and only 9 days remain. Residual probability accounts for Trump's unpredictability and the slim chance of a smaller, unrelated sale being processed.

factors.description: Explicit formal pause announced May 22 by acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao: the $14B Taiwan arms sale is paused to ensure munitions for Iran war operations ('Epic Fury'). No timeline for resumption given.
Trump personally undecided and treating Taiwan arms sales as a 'very good negotiating chip' with China; said May 15 he's 'unsure', May 21 he'd talk to Lai first but hasn't decided
Post-summit diplomatic dynamics: Trump-Xi summit May 13-15 produced warm tone and trade deals worth ~$17B; pushing Taiwan arms sale immediately would undermine diplomatic goodwill
5+ months of zero Taiwan FMS notifications since Dec 17, 2025; no other Taiwan sale appears to be in the pipeline besides the paused $14B package
Only 9 days remain in the window (May 22-31); even if Trump reversed course immediately, bureaucratic processing of notification through DSCA/State would take additional days
Strong bipartisan Congressional pressure to proceed: senators urging sales, House Foreign Affairs Committee clearing arms sale bills
Trump's unpredictability: could reverse course suddenly after talking with Lai Ching-te; Hung Cao acknowledged US has 'plenty' of munitions, suggesting pause could be lifted quickly

reasoning:
**Forecast: 4% probability (NO is very likely)**

## Key Reasoning

**The question** asks whether any new Congressional notification of a Taiwan arms sale will be posted on DSCA or State Department pages between May 17-31, 2026.

**Overwhelming evidence points to NO:**

1. **Formal pause announced today (May 22):** Acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao told Congress the $14B Taiwan arms sale is paused to ensure munitions sufficiency for Operation Epic Fury (the Iran war). No timeline for resumption.

2. **Trump personally undecided:** He called Taiwan arms sales a "very good negotiating chip" with China, said he's "unsure" about the package (May 15), and wants to talk to President Lai first (May 21) before deciding.

3. **Post-summit dynamics:** The Trump-Xi summit (May 13-15) produced warm diplomatic tone and ~$17B in trade deals. Pushing a Taiwan arms sale within days would undermine that goodwill.

4. **No pipeline:** No new Taiwan notification has been posted in 5+ months (since Dec 17, 2025). No other Taiwan sale appears to be in preparation.

5. **Only 9 days left:** Even if Trump reversed course immediately, the bureaucratic processing through DSCA/State would consume additional time.

The small 4% residual accounts for Trump's unpredictability and the theoretical possibility of a smaller, unrelated sale being processed.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the U.S. government formally notify Congress of a new arms sale to Taiwan between May 17 and May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43570)

[Trump Administration Is Pausing $14 Billion Taiwan Arms Sale To 'Make Sure' US Has Enough Munitions For I](https://www.benzinga.com/news/politics/26/05/52736986/trump-pauses-taiwan-arms-sale-iran-war-munitions)
[No notification from US on pause to €12bn arms sale, Taiwan says](https://www.euronews.com/2026/05/22/taiwan-says-it-has-received-no-notification-from-us-of-any-pause-to-planned-12bn-arms-sale)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between 00:00 UTC on May 17, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on May 31, 2026, the U.S. government publishes at least one new Congressional notification of a Foreign Military Sale (or Direct Commercial Sale notification under the Arms Export Control Act) to Taiwan — identified by the buyer 'Taipei Economic and Cultural Representative Office in the United States (TECRO)' or explicitly described as a sale to Taiwan — on either the DSCA Major Arms Sales page (https://www.dsca.mil/Press-Media/Major-Arms-Sales) or the State Department's Arms Sales: Congressional Notifications page (https://www.state.gov/arms-sales-congressional-notifications/). The notification's posted date (not the underlying certification/transmittal date) determines the window. Otherwise the question resolves NO. If both pages are unavailable on May 31, 2026, reporting by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) confirming a new Taiwan FMS Congressional notification published in the window will also resolve YES.
fine_print: Amendments, modifications, or re-postings of previously notified Taiwan cases do not count; the notification must be a new case (new case number or clearly described as a new sale). Public statements, leaks, intentions to sell, or congressional letters urging sales do not count — only an official posted Congressional notification suffices. Sales of services-only contracts (e.g., training, logistics support) to TECRO count if posted as a formal Congressional notification within the window.

factors.description: Explicit formal pause announced May 22 by acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao: the $14B Taiwan arms sale is paused to ensure munitions for Iran war operations
Trump personally undecided and treating Taiwan arms sales as a 'very good negotiating chip' with China; said on May 15 he's 'unsure' and on May 21 he'd talk to Lai first
Post-summit diplomatic dynamics: Trump-Xi summit May 13-15 produced warm tone and trade deals; pushing Taiwan arms sale immediately would undermine goodwill
5+ months of zero Taiwan FMS notifications (since Dec 17, 2025); no other Taiwan sale appears to be in the pipeline besides the paused $14B package
Only 9 days remain in the window (May 22-31); even if Trump reversed course immediately, bureaucratic processing of notification would take days
Strong bipartisan Congressional pressure to proceed with Taiwan arms sales; House Foreign Affairs Committee clearing arms sale bills
Trump's unpredictability: could reverse course suddenly, especially after talking with Lai Ching-te

input.anchor: Base rate for Taiwan FMS notification in any given 15-day window: historically ~10-15% based on clustering pattern (Nov-Dec 2025 had 9 notifications in ~5 weeks, but then 5 months of nothing). Current context makes this far below base rate.
input.assessment: The evidence overwhelmingly points to NO. The key development is the formal pause announced TODAY (May 22) by acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao, citing munitions needs for the Iran war. This is on top of Trump's personal indecision and his stated intent to use Taiwan arms sales as leverage with China. The $14B package — the only major candidate for notification — is explicitly on hold with no timeline for resumption. No other Taiwan sale appears to be in the pipeline. Even the unconditional base rate for a Taiwan notification in any 15-day window is modest, and all the current signals strongly point against any notification before May 31. The only residual probability comes from Trump's unpredictability and the theoretical possibility of a small, unrelated Taiwan sale being processed.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is ~12% (unconditional base rate for Taiwan notification in 15-day window based on historical frequency). All factors strongly push toward NO. Final estimate of 4% reflects near-certainty that no notification will be posted, with small residual for Trump unpredictability or unexpected bureaucratic action on a smaller sale.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Could Trump suddenly reverse course after talking to Lai? 2. Is there any smaller Taiwan sale in the pipeline that could be processed independently? 3. Could the 'pause' be lifted quickly if munitions review is completed?
input.tool_audit: Research tool: excellent, returned comprehensive data on both queries. Web search: very useful, found the critical May 22 pause announcement. Fetch_url: successfully retrieved details from Benzinga and Euronews articles. DSCA page returned 403 (expected, their site blocks automated access). Metaculus question fetch: successful.
input.update_triggers: 1. Trump announces he'll proceed with the $14B sale 2. DSCA posts any new Taiwan notification 3. Reports of a different, smaller Taiwan sale being prepared 4. Trump-Lai phone call producing concrete commitments
input.process_reflection: The research tool performed excellently here, providing comprehensive coverage of both the historical context and very recent developments. The web search complemented it well by surfacing the breaking news about the formal pause announced today. The multi-source triangulation (Benzinga, UPI, Al Jazeera, Euronews all confirming the pause) gave high confidence. The DSCA page being 403-blocked is a minor frustration but the research sub-agent apparently accessed it successfully earlier.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Factual Claims — All Verified

**Factor 1 (Hung Cao pause, logit=-2.5):** The trace contains multiple independent web search results (Benzinga, UPI, Al Jazeera, Euronews) and a successful fetch_url of the Benzinga article confirming acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao announced a pause to the $14B Taiwan arms sale during a Senate Appropriations Defense Subcommittee hearing on Thursday May 22, 2026. His direct quote is confirmed: "Right now we're doing a pause in order to make sure we have the munitions we need for Epic Fury — which we have plenty." Solidly grounded.

**Factor 2 (Trump undecided, logit=-1.5):** AP News confirms Trump "not yet made a decision"; Taipei Times May 21 confirms Trump said he'd speak with Lai; the Metaculus question background confirms Trump said he was "unsure" on May 15. All grounded.

**Factor 3 (Post-summit dynamics, logit=-1.0):** Multiple sources (Reuters, BBC, Politico) in the research result confirm the May 13-15 summit's warm tone and trade deal outcomes. Grounded.

**Factor 4 (5+ months gap, logit=-1.0):** Research result explicitly states "No new DSCA congressional notifications for Taiwan arms sales were issued in May 2026" and confirms the most recent was Dec 17, 2025. Grounded.

**Factor 5 (9 days remaining, logit=-0.8):** Simple arithmetic from publish date (May 22) to window end (May 31). Correct.

**Factors 6-7 (Congressional pressure and Trump unpredictability):** Both supported by trace evidence — House Foreign Affairs Committee bills, bipartisan support, Trump's stated intent to talk with Lai.

### No Double-Counting Issues
Factors 1 and 2 draw on different sources (Cao's institutional action vs. Trump's personal statements). Factors 2 and 3 overlap mildly (summit context feeds Trump's indecision) but capture distinct dimensions. No factor pair relies on the exact same underlying evidence.

### No Wrong-Direction Factors
All negative-logit factors argue against notification; all positive-logit factors argue for. Directions match descriptions.

### Resolution Criteria — Properly Engaged
The agent fetched the Metaculus question, obtained full resolution criteria, and correctly focuses on whether any *new* Congressional notification (not just the $14B package) will be posted. The assessment explicitly addresses that "the question doesn't require the $14B package specifically — any new Taiwan FMS notification counts."

### Tool Audit
The agent reports "DSCA page returned 403 (expected, their site blocks automated access)." The ground truth shows 1 error in 10 calls (the DSCA 403). The agent's characterization is fair — a 403 from a government site blocking scraping is expected, not a tool malfunction.

### Counterargument Quality — Adequate
The counterargument identifies concrete alternative scenarios: Trump talking to Lai and committing, a smaller unrelated sale in the pipeline, Cao's own admission that munitions are "plenty," and the Dec 2025 precedent of rapid batch notifications. It also specifies 5 quantified trigger scenarios. This is a genuine self-challenge.

### Probability Assessment — Appropriate
Given the formal pause announced the same day, Trump's personal indecision, post-summit diplomatic constraints, 5+ months of no notifications, no other sale in the pipeline, and only 9 days remaining, 4% is well-calibrated. The agent acknowledges the raw factor sum implies ~0.3% but rounds up to 4% for residual unpredictability, which is a reasonable calibration adjustment. My independent estimate would be 2-5%, so 4% falls squarely within range.

### Confidence (0.80) — Reasonable
Strong, current evidence from multiple independent sources. The only real uncertainty is Trump's unpredictability, which the agent appropriately accounts for in both the confidence level and the probability itself.

No errors found.
